speaker: interviewer
text: --[GERMAN] in Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, she was a Polish woman. And there was a lot of selections going on in Majdanek. And she always used to hide me in a bed and cover up the bed with a blanket. And the SS women used to come and look, and I was hidden. Twice she did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I must also tell you when we came to Majdanek, my mother and I, what shall I tell you? We were still-- we still didn't know what hit us. They used to bring food to Majdanek in-- in big kettles, you know? And women-- and they had big things, like carries-- you know, carriers with potatoes in their-- in their jacket.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the women used to fall into the food and smash the potatoes and so-- you know, the soup wold splash. And-- and my mother and I, we looked at each other, and we thought, we can't do that. We can-- we can't-- we can't run into food like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But after two, three days, we were so hungry, you know, they especially didn't make any order so people would behave like animals, you know? They were so hungry. Hungry-- hunger was driving them. Because later on, they gave everybody their portion. And that's how it should have been in the beginning. But they did it in a bestial way so everybody would be, you know, running.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, a man from the men camp-- the men were bringing bread to the women's camp. And one day, a man came and said he was looking for Mrs.-- well, in Polish-- Hasman, and her daughter. My mother and myself. And he came and he said he's got regards from my father. And he brought a bread. He brought his-- a little portion of bread which I know that my father didn't eat to send to us. And I know it was from my father because my father gave a message that said like that. He-- he is to kiss my mother from my father, and he is to take a long, long look at me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I knew it was from my father. It was the last time I heard from my father. And of course, you understand, that my brother being a child was gassed in Majdanek all by himself. Can you understand what I'm saying to you? He went to his death, a child of seven, all by himself, because my father was obviously in the camp. They must have separated them. Without anybody. He died there in gas chamber. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was the last message I had from my father. We were three months in Majdanek. And as I said to you, they were sending people-- grabbing people to work in [GERMAN], which was a garden. And I don't know, but I somehow or other, from my young age, I became very, very clever, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe the circumstances. I became-- the instinct how to save myself. So when they grab me to this work, I used to take a spade in the morning and walk around with a spade and do nothing, pretending-- every time I saw an SS coming, I pretended I was working. And they were growing there peas. I used to-- my mother bought me, as I told you, this long frock. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was too big for me. With a belt-- with a tie. And I used to put a lot of peas in there. And when I marched in at nighttime, I used to sell the peas for bread. The-- the-- the people, like the [GERMAN] and the kapos, they used to have plenty of bread. And they needed something nicer. So that's how I used to sell it. And we had more than one portion of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was a very bad camp, Majdanek. They-- they were forever hanging people and beating people and they were-- I can't even tell you. For this-- without any reason at all, they would grab somebody and-- and-- and-- and beat them up, you know? And we would-- were hanging two women, I don't know why.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was one very sadistic woman from Lithuania who used to go with a horse whip and used to whack-- you know, beat everybody up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, one day, they made-- they said for everybody to congregate in the camp. And they said that they going to send transports away from Majdanek. I think they must have been liquidating Majdanek. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And how shall I put it to you? They took 2,000 women, and I had a-- made another selection. And I had a lot of trouble too-- that time too. I was very lucky with the cigarettes, because there was again a doctor who was making the selection. And my mother went through, and he started smoking a cigarette just as I was going to come through the selection. And my mother grabbed me by the hand and pulled me towards her because they were again putting people to one side to death, and people to go to the transports. And there were again Polish women notice what happened, and they quickly put my number down to go to the transport. 2,000 women they picked up that day. And they-- we marched. And they marched us to a man camp. And I don't know why they march us to a man camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we were sitting there on the floor in the-- in the men's camp when all of a sudden there came an order for us to get up, and they were-- we marching. And behind us was going ambulance, a Red Cross ambulance. And I was thinking to myself, isn't it nice of them to have an ambulances in case somebody gets sick or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they marched us to gas chambers. Only I didn't know at the time it was gas chambers. As a matter of fact, a few years ago, I met a lady who was in my transport and we talked about it. 2,000 of us. And they put us in gas chambers. I-- I remember it looked to me like a sauna, because they were, you know, the showers coming from the ceiling. There was no windows. And there was a door. And a little window like you see above, you know. Very narrow little window. And my mother and I sat near the door. And we sat there and sat there. And we sat there the whole night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We thought that any minute-- we're so stupid that we still didn't know where we were. Of course, we heard about gassing, but we didn't know that the gas is coming through the shower like it-- made to look like shower so people wouldn't panic, you know? And that's where the gas was coming through. And the-- I mentioned to you that the-- that the ambulance-- it was not an ambulance. They were carrying the gas, which I-- we didn't know either. And we were sitting there a whole night. And in the morning, the door opened. An SS man opened the door. And my mother and I were sitting near the door. And he said to us in German, children, he said, you have been saved. And they march us back to the camp where the men were. And when we came there, we found out that two men hanged themselves because in our transport were their daughters, and they knew that we went to gas chambers. See, the men in the camp back-- they knew. Anyway, soon after they marched us to a railway. And they put us on a train. And we went to Birkenau. And on the train, we had-- each carriage had a postman. And this-- we had a-- a-- a sadistic one. He was shooting to the girls at random, and he killed quite a few girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. You had a-- beg your pardon. A what? A post?

speaker: interviewee
text: A post. They used to call them a post. A-- a-- a German guard. A German guard was sitting. And he was a very sadistic one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just say that name again, please? A-- a post.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was called a post-- a postman. Something like that. I don't remember anymore. I got myself now confused. It will come to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in-- he was in the carriage with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- in every carriage there was one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were traveling.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yes. And he was shooting at random. And at one stage, I thought he killed me because I was sitting not far from him. But he killed the girl next to me. And anyway, we arrived after this-- this-- this terrible, terrible journey in this train. We arrived in Birkenau. And they put us-- they always put us in the rows of five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still with my mother. And they put us-- otherwise, I wouldn't have survived. We only survived because we were together. I'm sure I wouldn't have survived because all the time, throughout my being in camp, my-- my fear that I will be separated from my mother was so great that I can't even describe it to you. And that fear remained with me for the rest of my life till my mother died.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- the minute she was sick, I was in-- in-- in extreme panic. I was always scared that she will go, you know? Die. And anyway, we marched in towards-- into Birkenau. It was a big gate. And I'm not even mentioning to you that all the time, the treatment that we received was brutal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And screaming and yelling and-- and beating, and-- and giving-- and-- and creating a terrible fear in us all the time. We arrived at the gate. And I-- I-- we didn't know where we arrive, because all of a sudden a music started playing. There was an orchestra sitting at the gate playing waltzes. So we looked at each other and we thought, after all this, where-- where have they taken us? You know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it didn't last long to-- that was a orchestra made of women who-- Jewish women-- who were in the camp. And I befriended one Viennese girl who used to played violin. And she used to invite me-- they had a special block for themselves, and they had rehearsals. And I used to come and watch the rehearsals. And it was lovely because I had a bit of music. I befriended this girl who was only a little bit older than I. Anyway, the beginning in-- in-- in-- in Birkenau, we were in Lager A, A. We were hungry all the time and cold and miserable. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- we really never ever dreamt that we are going to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything happen to you when you arrived in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. They put us into-- we march into this camp. And they march us into the barracks. They had those barracks. And straightaway, they gave us beds where to sleep, and a dish made of, I think, enamel. And a spoon. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you tattooed? Were you-- did you-- were you given a tattoo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was given it too.

speaker: interviewer
text: A number.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I forgot about that. I'm glad you reminded me. I forgot about that. This is a very vital part of the story because when we arrived in Auschwitz, they took us to a hall. And they said-- first of all, they cut our hair off completely. We had no hair. They cut our hair off, and we were like, you know, with a machine-- little machine, they took the rest off. And I had long braids that my mother used to make a crown of, you know, to make me look older. And when they did it to my mother, I started crying. And she said to me-- Lusia, she said to me-- I'll never forget it-- don't cry, she said. Because if we will have our heads, we'll get new hair. And then they started tattoo-- put-- you know, putting tattoo on our arms. And again, I was petrified. And my mother said, Lusia, I'll be first so you will see it doesn't hurt at all. And she had 48,702. And I've got 48,703. And they said to us like this. Children who are in the transport, if there are any, there is a special children's block in Birkenau. And the children will get white bread and milk. And my mother said to me, Lusia, you ought to go to this barrack and stay there because you will get good food. And I remembered what my father said, that you do exactly the opposite what the Germans said. So I said, Mama, I'm not going. And sure enough, after I was in the camp, I used to go and visit this block where the children were. They were there four or five weeks, a full block of children.

speaker: interviewer
text: One morning, I went to see them. And in the night, they took them to gas chambers. And they gassed them all. One night. You understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had tortures for-- for us, I can't even tell you. They make us run around naked through the whole camp, all of us. Backwards and forwards. I don't know why they did that. Anyway. But that-- that happened in-- in-- in the-- yes. That was still happening in the-- in the block-- in the camp A. And then they got us to clean up the B lager. And the B lager was right next door to the A lager. And the B lager was empty. There were people there, but they-- they probably killed them. And it was full of fleas. And I remember I was called to clean it, and it was terrible. Terrible. But we cleaned it up. And as soon as we clean up the camp, which took quite a few weeks, they shifted us to the other camp B. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just-- just to clarify. When did you arrive in Auschwitz? Do you-- do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. July, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And so you first went in one camp, and now you're going to another camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was-- the 8th of May, we had arrived in Majdanek, 1943. And July. So we were from May give or take few days for the-- for the journey. We-- in July we arrived in-- in-- in Birkenau. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were going to the new camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: New camp. Camp B.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Camp B. And my mother developed malaria. A lot of people had malaria in-- in Birkenau. There was a lot of swamps and-- and mud. And must have been mosquitoes breeding. I don't know. She got malaria. And every night-- every day at the same time, she used to get an attack. And I need-- needed quinine for my mother. And the going rate for quinine was for one tablet, a loaf of bread. Well, I haven't seen a loaf of bread, I don't know for how long. You know, because we used to get those portions. When I was talking, one woman said to me like that. You know, she said, not far there is a-- the gas chambers are. And she pointed to me. It was really very close to the barrack I was in. I could-- just across the road. And it was-- was-- you know, there were wires. Barbed wires, electric wires. And there was watch houses. You know, watchtowers. And she said, if you-- if you were to go and talk to the man from the gas chambers, there is a lot of transports coming. And they have everything there. I'm sure they have quinine. And if you were to go and talk to them, maybe you'll get quinine for your mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went. And we were not allowed. There was a ditch, electric wires. And I was on-- we were not allowed to come anywhere near the ditch or the electric wires. But I went because I needed it for my mother. So I was standing in the ditch and a man passed across the wires. When did you go? At nighttime, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you go across the ditch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: When.

speaker: interviewee
text: When? One day.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the middle of the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just one day in the middle of the day, yes. Because at nighttime, we were not allowed to walk in the camp. And this man, he was Jewish. And I said to him-- look, I said to him. Somebody told me that you've got everything here, and I need quinine for my mother. In Polish it's called [POLISH]. And I said to him-- spoke to him in Polish. I said to him, my mother is very sick. She's got malaria. I need quinine for my mother. So he said to me, wait here. I'll send you out a doctor. And he sent me a man. And this man looked at me and he said, you know-- he spoke to me. And he said to me, you remind me very much of my little girl, of my daughter. He said, my name is Chaim Kaminski. I am not a-- a doctor. I'm the kapo of the gas chambers, of the Sonderkommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said to me, look. I will take care of you, he said. Which block are you in? And I told him. And he said, go back to your block and don't ever come back here because if a [GERMAN]-- that they were called, [GERMAN]. If the [GERMAN] will see you, the SS on the-- on the watchtower, they'll shoot you. So don't come here anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went back to my barrack. And few hours later, he sent over a whole bottle of quinine for my mother, and a piece of cake for me, and a little toy, like a little-- how you call it. A little-- that you pull strings. A puppet. That you pull strings. And to the [GERMAN], he sent a lot of things. He sent to her perfume. See, they had it all from the transports that were coming. You understand, in gas chambers. And he-- he send to her soap and-- and-- and perfume, and other things, and told her to see that she doesn't send me or my mother to [GERMAN], and that she-- she takes good care of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the [GERMAN]?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [GERMAN] was a-- a Slovakian Jewess.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: All-- no. All [GERMAN] in Birkenau were Slovakian Jewesses. And they were the toughest of the toughest. Because if-- if 50,000 Slovakian Jewesses came, maybe 100 survived. And they had to be the toughest of the toughest, do you understand? That was the ratio of survival. And none of the even to exaggeration.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she-- she really-- not that bad-- took not that bad care of us because [GERMAN] was like a death sentence, you see? You-- whether it was hot or cold, you stayed there a whole day. What is [GERMAN]? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN] meant-- means-- it's a commander that works outside. That is the literal translation. [GERMAN] is outside. And you were there either in the heat the whole day, and that's what happened to a lot of women in Majdanek. They got gangrene because they used to put us in the sun for a whole day, and they had-- they had third degree burns and they wouldn't give them any medicine, so they got gangrene and they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the same was in Birkenau. Or they put you in the cold, that you couldn't survive either because it was so cold. A whole day, from [GERMAN] to [GERMAN]. You know what [GERMAN] means? Yes. Well, could you explain for the interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, [GERMAN] was a roll call, which was in the morning at 5 o'clock they used to take you out of the barrack outside and give you a bit of black water, hot water to drink, what they called coffee. And they used to keep you there for two, three hours to count you. But the-- the idea was to-- to exhaust you, to stand there for two, three hours so that you will be completely exhausted before the day started. And when you came back, they did the same. And at the end of the day, they gave you a bit of soup. But you also stayed there for few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did your-- what did you do for work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was just about to come to that. I was again talking to the people. And one woman said to me, obviously you have a bit of a luck, and you-- obviously you have a bit of a connections in the camp, she said. Because you don't go to [GERMAN]. This-- somebody is helping you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you ask to get a job? It would be very good for you to get a job. I-- I think that those women, really-- why would they tell me a thing like that? It was all meant to be-- I really-- I'm a believer, you know? I-- I read somewhere that when you witness terrible atrocities in life, terrible cruelty, you either turn away from God altogether or you become a believe. Do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think that's what happened to me. And I went again to this Mr-- to this Kaminski, Chaim Kaminski, who said to me that after the war, if we survive, he's going to adopt me and we'll go and live in Palestine. Because he lost his daughter, he wants to adopt me, and I'll be his daughter. I went again to him, and I said, look, I'd like to have a job. And he said, all right. I'll see that you get a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to him though he told you not to go to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. But it was an important thing for me. That was the two times I was there. Look, you had to take risk if you wanted to survive. And he had a girlfriend in the camp. You wouldn't believe it. She was the kapo from [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] was a-- a-- a store of clothing that came with the transport of people. And it was segregate-- it was like-- not segregate. It's not the right expression for it. But it was, you know-- how should I put it. The good stuff went to Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was-- what is this expression. Not segregated. There were, you know-- the good stuff, they sent to Germany. And the bad stuff, they kept for the-- for the people in-- in the camp to wear. And I-- and that's what it was all about, you know. And he sent me with a big cigarette holder and a cigarette to this kapo. And he said, you give [GERMAN], you give regards, to Frau Schmidt, who is a kapo. She was a Czechoslovakian woman. Not Jewish. She was there politically because she was a secretary to, I think, Dr. Benes in Czechoslovakia. One of the ministers in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he send me to her. Why I'm telling you this story is because she saved my life many times. And she said to me, well, what is it that you like? I-- I gave her the regards from him. She was his girlfriend because when they used to come from the gas chambers, they used to come once a week or perhaps more often from the gas chambers and crematorium to collect dead bodies.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the men used to come, understand. And that's how they used to meet, the women and the men. That's how she was his girlfriend. She had a room of her own. She was a very privileged halfling. Halfling is a-- a prisoner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And because she was the kapo and she was the-- she had the whole [GERMAN], you know. She was dressing up everybody in camp, all the new transports and so on. And she says, well, what kind of a job do you want? I said-- no. She said to me, what did you come? Would you like some clothes, she said? Well, here you are. Take whatever you like. I said, no. I said, I don't want any clothes. She said, what is it that you want? She said, I want a job here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard it was a very good job there too. So she said to me-- she started laughing because I was a little girl. And then she said to me, come tomorrow. I'll see what I can do for you. I'll speak to the SS woman, the [GERMAN]. When I came the next day, she said to me, all right. You have a job here. You're going to be our messenger girl. And because of that, my mother and I survived. You see, I was never sent to selections when I worked for her. And the idea of survival was that it was an enormous store. Enormous. The idea was that you took out things without their knowledge, without nobody's knowledge, and you sold it. You sold it to people who had bread and food, like the [GERMAN] and the-- and the kapos, and people who were influential prisoners, halflings. They had it. And I used to smuggle out the clothing every single day. And I used to bribe the [GERMAN] in a block not to send my mother to [GERMAN]. And then I got her a job in a [GERMAN], which was a sewing-- a sewing place. I always-- I looked after my mother because the luck for survival was coming through me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I looked after her, not she after me. Do you understand? We had-- we had food to eat. We were very lucky. Because-- because I was smuggling the things out. And one day, I was caught smuggling it. And she threw me out. Who threw you out?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kapo. Schmidt. She threw me out. And now, my-- my-- all my trouble started, because now I wasn't anymore protected from selections. And there was a big selection in the camp. And my mother-- naked. And we block by block had to go to selection. And Dr. Mengele was the one that was making the selection. And again, they put on one side the people to live and on the other side the people to die. And again, my mother was put on the side to live, and I was put-- my number was taken down on the side to die, because I was a child. And I return to my barrack. And again, this is a third time I went to the-- to the wire to tell Chaim Kaminski that I have been-- my number has been taken down to go to the gas chambers in the selection. So he said to me, don't you worry, he said to me. When you come to the gas chambers, I'll get you out. But there were five gas chambers, and I didn't think that he will be in every gas chamber looking for me. So I told to myself, this is it. I'm going to die. And he must have somehow told the kapo, the Frau Schmidt, that I have been taken-- my number has been taken down to gas chambers. And she was a very, very influential person in the camp. She called me. She sent somebody to call me. And she said to me-- look, she said to me. We spoke-- she spoke-- I learned by the time to speak perfect Czechoslovakian. And-- it's very similar to Polish. And she said to me, look, I will get you out. There will be lists. I'll-- I'll get your name crossed off. Don't you worry. Go back to your barrack, and I will get your name crossed off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
